old_claim.note: Public Key Infrastructure provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Digital identity systems can authenticate with passwords, shared secrets, one-time-password seeds, biometrics, or asymmetric keys, so PKI is not universally required.

source_shape.source_locator: Digital Identity Model, authentication factors and authenticator-secrets discussion: secrets may be asymmetric key pairs or shared secrets including passwords, symmetric keys, and OTP seeds.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: NIST's digital identity model supports several authentication architectures and explicitly includes both public-key and non-public-key authenticators.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because password, OTP, shared-secret, and biometric-backed systems satisfy the model without certificate authorities or trust chains, PKI is an optional architecture choice rather than a direct dependency.

ontology_before: The graph made all digital identity systems depend directly on one certificate-based authentication architecture.
ontology_after: Digital identity remains architecture-neutral and may use PKI-backed authenticators or independent shared-secret, OTP, and biometric approaches.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-pki-independent-identity-and-primitive-boundaries: digital_identity_systems no longer directly depends on public_key_infrastructure.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed specifically to certificate-based digital identities whose authenticators cannot function without an X.509 trust chain.
short_reason: NIST explicitly models both asymmetric-key and shared-secret authenticators, so PKI is not a universal digital-identity prerequisite.
